464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding & first 30 days**: zero documented pathway from sign-up through goal-setting, intro class, form checks, retention touchpoints — this is your highest-churn window
- **Churn prevention & win-back**: no formal process for pause requests, cancellation conversations, re-engagement campaigns, or exit data capture
- **Coaching standards & quality**: no audit checklist, observation protocol, or feedback loop — inconsistent class experience kills retention
- **Coach development**: no roadmap for progression, skill gaps, or upskilling — limits team depth and member trust
- **Member milestone tracking & outcomes**: no mechanism to celebrate progress, demonstrate ROI, or trigger upsells (30-day goals, transformation tracking, milestone comms)
- **Health & safety incident protocol**: no complaints, injury reporting, safeguarding, or GDPR breach response (critical for women's-only space; reputational risk)
- **Supply chain**: no ordering SOP for stock, sundries, or equipment — ad hoc buying kills cash flow and service gaps
- **Referral programme ops**: no mechanics, tracking, or fulfilment process (easy lever for organic growth, currently leaving money on table)
- **Troubleshooting guide**: no escalation path for common member issues (cancellation resistance, form anxiety, billing disputes, coach conflict)

**Needs updating**

- **Marketing SOPs (5/5 exist but...)**: likely missing member communication cadence (email/SMS/app sequences for onboarding, lapsed, VIP retention), referral prompts, seasonal campaign triggers
- **Coach weekly tasks**: no SOP yet — needs clarity on class prep, member check-ins, feedback logs, floor coverage, and handover protocols
- **New quarter programme launch**: no SOP — needs template for class schedule comms, marketing pre-launch, coach

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Pricing is under-deployed.** AR/M has collapsed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June) — a 29% drop despite headcount rising 18 members. You've grown the studio *and* diluted unit revenue. Implement tiered membership tiers (Basic £25/month, Premium £35 with class booking priority, Elite £50 with PT credits) to re-segment 147 members and recover to £155+ AR/M by end of Q3. This alone adds £5k+/month at no volume cost.

• **Retention collapsed Q2 and remains poor.** January–April showed 5.74–8.33% churn; June hit 0% (suspicious or data gap?). But May–June member growth was only +19 despite a doubled studio — you're leaking members faster than new intake fills seats. Introduce a 90-day onboarding program, exit interview follow-ups for churned members, and a referral bonus (£15 credit for each friend who signs up) to hit 3% churn max. At 147 members, 3% churn vs 5.74% saves ~14 members/month = £1,800+ retained revenue.

• **Non-peak class slots are empty.** A doubled studio means fixed costs scale but utilisation must follow. Run a 2-week attendance audit by class/time slot (early morning, midday, evening). Offer "Quiet Hour" discounts (50% off) or free drop-in taster sessions at 9am or 2pm weekdays. Target: fill 70% of all class slots; currently you're likely at 50–55%.

• **Upsell PT and retail hard.** With 147 members, even 10% uptake of 1×PT session/month at £40 = £588/month incremental. Stock branded merch (water bottles, gym wear, protein) and offer members 20% discount; typical margin 40% on £500/month retail turnover = £200 profit. Train all staff to pitch PT in induction and post-class.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people expenses are the crisis.** June hit £9,390 (52.9% of revenue); May was £9,233. Excluding April's relocation, this is 52–53% of turnover vs 28–32% in Sep–Nov. Drill into the detail: rent for the new studio is likely higher, but utilities, supplies, and contractor costs need forensic review. Request itemised P&

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **JoJo is overloaded at 133 sessions.** Move her 44 PT slots to Eilis (who has capacity at 48 sessions) and redistribute some of her Build: Upper classes. This brings JoJo closer to sustainable levels and develops Eilis's PT skills.

• **Grace's Build: Lower (17 classes) must shift to Eilis.** Eilis is ready for non-Tuesday strength work. Start with 2–3 sessions weekly to build her confidence, then scale up through July.

• **Grace's Elevate (6 classes) and Strongevity (3 classes) are lightly attended and should consolidate.** Merge these into a single weekly "Elevate+" slot and assign to Eilis or JoJo depending on demand; this reduces Grace's timetable immediately.

• **Grace's No Sweat Intro (3 sessions) should become a rolling process.** Train Eilis to deliver intros as part of onboarding, or batch them into one monthly group session—remove them from the weekly timetable entirely.

• **Body Composition scans are a drain on coaching time.** Delegate all scans (currently 40 across the team) to a reception or admin role, or ask members to self-scan with brief verbal guidance. This frees ~5–6 coaching hours per month across the team.

• **Review JoJo's Tuesday load once Grace steps back.** She's carrying all technically demanding work. Once Eilis has shadowed and grown, trial her on a modified Tuesday (e.g. Circuits or Elevate) to spread risk and prevent burnout.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio gaps:**

1. **Add a 30-min "Open Gym + Coach" slot** on Monday and Thursday evenings (your quietest days) — members can drop in to use equipment with a coach available for form checks or mini-consultations; charge £5-8 per session to generate revenue from otherwise empty studio time.

2. **Launch a monthly "Barbell Basics" workshop** (60 mins) on a quiet Thursday or Monday, targeting newer members who might find Barbelle intimidating; position it as a low-pressure intro that feeds people into the class later.

**For quieter classes themselves:**

3. **Move Engine to a prime slot** (Tuesday or Wednesday morning/lunchtime when overall gym traffic is highest) and run a 2-week "Try Engine free" campaign with a targeted invite to Upper/Full Body Build attendees — the data suggests your strongest audience isn't sampling it yet, so it's a timing and awareness problem, not a class problem.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Build for The Forge

**Map your current revenue streams ruthlessly**
- Audit every service you actually deliver: group classes, personal training, nutrition coaching, small-group training, corporate packages, virtual options. List actual prices members pay *now*. You'll spot gaps and pricing inconsistencies immediately.

**Create two separate documents—not one fancy menu**
- **Coaches' version**: Internal pricing, commission structure, package inclusions, upsell triggers, cancellation/refund policies. This stops your team inventing their own rules.
- **Members' version**: Clean, benefits-focused (what results/feeling they get), simple pricing tiers, comparison table showing what "Premium PT" vs "Group Class" includes. Remove internal jargon.

**Price strategically against your positioning**
- You're women-only and community-focused in Belfast—you're *not* competing on budget. Price 15-20% above generic gyms. Research 2-3 premium female-focused gyms in UK. Anchor your pricing there, not below.

**Build in deliberate upsell paths**
- Group class member → Intro to PT (subsidised first session). PT client → Nutrition add-on (+£25/month). Show the progression on your menu so coaches know what to offer when.

**Test with your current members first**
- Share draft menus with 5-10 loyal members before finalizing. They'll tell you what's confusing and what's missing. Use their feedback to refine pricing/bundling within 48 hours.

What revenue stream are you currently under-pricing or not promoting?

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has quadrupled since April (£6 → £27) while absolute lead volume has collapsed 94% (69 → 4). The spend reduction to £109 looks reactive rather than strategic—you're getting fewer leads at worse efficiency, not optimising down. However, your close rate has actually *improved* to 75% (vs 60% in April), suggesting lead quality hasn't tanked; the surviving leads are better. July's profitability is razor-thin because volume disappeared.

The trend screams ad fatigue + audience saturation. You've likely exhausted your core Belfast women-fitness audience repeatedly across four months. Meta's algorithm has cycled through everyone genuinely interested, leaving only expensive, marginal prospects. The creative or targeting stopped resonating months ago.

**Actions**

1. **Rebuild audience architecture from scratch.** Stop running to your existing saved audiences. Test three parallel campaigns next month: (a) Lookalike audiences built from your best customers (25+ close rate), (b) interest-stacking (Women 25–45 + Fitness + Wellness, excluding prior clickers), (c) geographic expansion (Dublin day-trippers, women in surrounding postcodes). Allocate £25–30 to each to find which pulls sub-£15 CPL again.

2. **Overhaul creative immediately.** July's creatives are burnt out. Pull stats on which ads drove April's 69 leads—identify the top 2–3 performers. Remake those concepts with fresh footage (different trainer, testimonial format, before-afters). Test at least 4 new ad variations week one of August. Your 75% close rate tells us messaging works; reach is the problem, not positioning.

3. **Lift spend back to £350–400/month but only after testing.** Don't spend blind. Run the new audiences + creatives at £100 total for 5–7 days, measure CPL, then scale the winner 2–3x. July's low spend was a retreat; you need to re-enter with fresh ammunition. Aim to land back at £12–15 CPL by mid-August.

4. **Single biggest lever: switch from broad to looka

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## REVENUE — URGENT: Reverse the decline & member value collapse

• **Rev/member has crashed to £109 (June) from £138 (April).** This is your biggest red flag. Either member mix has shifted downmarket, class attendance per member is down, or card sales have dried up. Audit your June cohort: are new joiners on cheaper plans? Are existing members using fewer classes? **Action: Pull card-revenue breakdown from Stripe this week — if card sales <30% of total, you need a new upsell play (PT sessions, nutrition, retail) hitting every member at sign-up.**

• **Churn spiked to 11.9% in May** (worst month) and was 11.8% in December. Outside those outliers, you're holding 3–6%. **Action: Exit-survey every leaver immediately — are they price-sensitive, bored with class rotation, or life changes? If price, you have a segment problem, not a pricing problem.** Target 2% churn by Q3.

• **Revenue flat at £15–17k over last 3 months (Apr–Jun) vs prior 3 (Jan–Mar, average £14.5k).** Membership growth (105→145) is real, but underperforming: you should see £18–20k at 145 active members if Rev/member stayed at £130+. **Action: Freeze new member discounts. Introduce 6-month contracts (not rolling) to lock in value. Run a "Bring a Friend" cashback week — June churn at 3.2% shows good sticky cohorts; use them.**

---

## EXPENSES — Flag cash outflows; relocation is done, now tighten

• **Cash out in June (£20.6k) is still elevated and sits 19% above revenue (£15.8k).** Excluding the April relocation spike (£39k), your average cash out Jan–Jun (ex-April) is £18.5k vs £15.1k revenue — you're running a 1.22× burn ratio. **Action: Line-by-line P&L audit on payroll, rent (new lease cost post-relocation?), utilities, supplier fees. If rent jumped post-move, model a 10% efficiency gain (staff cross-training, energy controls) by end Q3 to get cash out below £16k.**

• **March and June both went negative on net cash (£-5.3k, £-2.5k) despite reasonable revenues.** This signals timing mismatches or a fixed-cost base that
